mf_abort.name: mf_abort
mf_abort.descriptionMore: Will abort with a straightforward %abort if the condition is true.
mf_abort.author: Allan Bowe

mf_dedup.name: mf_dedup
mf_dedup.descriptionMore: Removes all duplicates from a string of words.  A delimeter can be chosen.  Is inspired heavily by this excellent [macro]( https://github.com/scottbass/SAS/blob/master/Macro/dedup_mstring.sas) from [Scott Base](https://www.linkedin.com/in/scottbass).  Case sensitive.
mf_dedup.usage: %let str=One two one two and through and through; %put %mf_dedup(&str); %put %mf_dedup(&str,outdlm=%str(,)); Which returns: > One two one and through > One,two,one,and,through
mf_dedup.author: Allan Bowe

mf_deletefile.name: mf_deletefile
mf_deletefile.description: Deletes a physical file, if it exists
mf_deletefile.outputs: The return code from the fdelete() invocation
mf_deletefile.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.filepath: Full path to the target file

mf_existds.name: mf_existds
mf_existds.description: Checks whether a dataset OR a view exists.
mf_existds.descriptionMore: Can be used in open code, eg as follows: %if %mf_existds(libds=work.someview) %then %put  yes it does!; NOTE - some databases have case sensitive tables, for instance POSTGRES with the preserve_tab_names=yes libname setting.  This may impact expected results (depending on whether you 'expect' the result to be case insensitive in this context!)
mf_existds.author: Allan Bowe

mf_existfeature.name: mf_existfeature
mf_existfeature.descriptionMore: Check to see if a feature is supported in your environment. Run without arguments to see a list of detectable features. Note - this list is based on known versions of SAS rather than actual feature detection, as that is tricky / impossible to do without generating errs in most cases. %put %mf_existfeature(PROCLUA);
mf_existfeature.outputs: output returns 1 or 0 (or -1 if not found)
mf_existfeature.author: Allan Bowe

mf_existfileref.name: mf_existfileref
mf_existfileref.descriptionMore: You can probably do without this macro as it is just a one liner. Mainly it is here as a convenient way to remember the syntax!
mf_existfileref.outputs: output Returns 1 if found and 0 if not found.  Note - it is possible that the fileref is found, but the file does not (yet) exist. If you need to test for this, you may as well use the fileref function directly.
mf_existfileref.author: Allan Bowe

mf_existfunction.name: mf_existfunction
mf_existfunction.descriptionMore: Returns 1 if the function exists, else 0.  Note that this function can be slow as it needs to open the sashelp.vfuncs table.
mf_existfunction.usage: %put %mf_existfunction(CAT); %put %mf_existfunction(DOG); Full credit to [Bart](https://sasensei.com/user/305) for the vfunc pointer and the tidy approach for pure macro data set filtering. Check out his [SAS Packages](https://github.com/yabwon/SAS_PACKAGES) framework!  Where you can find the same [function]( https://github.com/yabwon/SAS_PACKAGES/blob/main/packages/baseplus.md#functionexists-macro ).
mf_existfunction.author: Allan Bowe

mf_existvar.name: mf_existvar
mf_existvar.description: Checks if a variable exists in a data set.
mf_existvar.descriptionMore: Returns 0 if the variable does NOT exist, and the position of the var if it does.
mf_existvar.author: Allan Bowe

mf_existvarlist.name: mf_existvarlist
mf_existvarlist.description: Checks if a set of variables ALL exist in a data set.
mf_existvarlist.descriptionMore: Returns 0 if ANY of the variables do not exist, or 1 if they ALL do.
mf_existvarlist.author: Allan Bowe

mf_fmtdttm.name: mf_fmtdttm
mf_fmtdttm.description: Returns E8601DT26.6 if compatible else DATETIME19.3
mf_fmtdttm.descriptionMore: From our experience in [Data Controller for SAS] (https://datacontroller.io) deployments, the E8601DT26.6 datetime format has the widest support when it comes to pass-through SQL queries. However, it is not supported in WPS or early versions of SAS 9 (M3 and below) when used as a datetime literal, eg: data _null_; demo="%sysfunc(datetime(),E8601DT26.6)"dt; demo=; run; This macro will therefore return DATEITME19.3 as an alternative format based on the runtime environment so that it can be used in such cases, eg: data _null_; demo="%sysfunc(datetime(),%mf_fmtdttm())"dt; demo=; run;
mf_fmtdttm.author: Allan Bowe

mf_getapploc.name: mf_getapploc
mf_getapploc.description: Returns the appLoc from the _program variable
mf_getapploc.descriptionMore: When working with SASjs apps, web services / tests / jobs are always deployed to a root (app) location in the SAS logical folder tree. When building apps for use in other environments, you do not necessarily know where the backend services will be deployed.  Therefore a function like this is handy in order to dynamically figure out the appLoc, and enable other services to be connected by a relative reference. SASjs apps always have the same immediate substructure (one or more of the following): @li /data @li /jobs @li /services @li /tests @li /tests/jobs @li /tests/services @li /tests/macros This function works by testing for the existence of any of the above in the automatic _program variable, and returning the part to the left of it.
mf_getapploc.author: Allan Bowe

mf_getattrc.name: mf_getattrc
mf_getattrc.description: Returns a character attribute of a dataset.
mf_getattrc.descriptionMore: Can be used in open code, eg as follows: %put Dataset label = %mf_getattrc(sashelp.class,LABEL); %put Member Type = %mf_getattrc(sashelp.class,MTYPE);
mf_getattrc.outputs: output returns result of the attrc value supplied, or -1 and log message if err.
mf_getattrc.author: Allan Bowe

mf_getattrn.name: mf_getattrn
mf_getattrn.description: Returns a numeric attribute of a dataset.
mf_getattrn.descriptionMore: Can be used in open code, eg as follows: %put Number of observations=%mf_getattrn(sashelp.class,NLOBS); %put Number of variables = %mf_getattrn(sashelp.class,NVARS);
mf_getattrn.outputs: output returns result of the attrn value supplied, or -1 and log message if err.
mf_getattrn.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.attr: Common values are NLOBS and NVARS, full list in [documentation]( http://support.sas.com/documentation/cdl/en/lrdict/64316/HTML/default/viewer.htm#a000212040.htm)

mf_getengine.name: mf_getengine
mf_getengine.description: Returns the engine type of a SAS library
mf_getengine.descriptionMore: Usage: %put %mf_getengine(SASHELP); returns: > V9 A note is also written to the log.  The credit for this macro goes to the contributors of Chris Hemedingers blog [post]( http://blogs.sas.com/content/sasdummy/2013/06/04/find-a-sas-library-engine/)
mf_getengine.outputs: output returns the library engine (uppercase) for the FIRST library encountered. @warning will only return the FIRST library engine - for concatenated libraries, with different engines, inconsistent results may be encountered.
mf_getengine.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.libref: Library reference (also accepts a 2 level libds ref).

mf_getfilesize.name: mf_getfilesize
mf_getfilesize.description: Returns the size of a file in bytes.
mf_getfilesize.descriptionMore: Provide full path/filename.extension to the file, eg: %put %mf_getfilesize(fpath=C:\temp\myfile.txt); or, provide a libds value as follows: data x;do x=1 to 100000;y=x;output;end;run; %put %mf_getfilesize(libds=work.x,format=yes); Which gives: > 2mb
mf_getfilesize.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.fpath=: Full path and filename.  Provide this OR the libds value.
parameters.libds=: (0) Library.dataset value (assumes library is BASE engine)
parameters.format=: (NO) Set to yes to apply sizekmg. format

mf_getfmtlist.name: mf_getfmtlist
mf_getfmtlist.description: Returns a distinct list of formats from a table
mf_getfmtlist.descriptionMore: Reads the dataset header and returns a distinct list of formats applied. %put NOTE- %mf_getfmtlist(sashelp.prdsale); %put NOTE- %mf_getfmtlist(sashelp.shoes); %put NOTE- %mf_getfmtlist(sashelp.demographics); returns: > DOLLAR $CHAR W MONNAME > $CHAR BEST DOLLAR > BEST Z $CHAR COMMA PERCENTN
mf_getfmtlist.author: Allan Bowe

mf_getfmtname.name: mf_getfmtname
mf_getfmtname.description: Extracts a format name from a fully defined format
mf_getfmtname.descriptionMore: Converts formats in like $thi3. and th13.2 $THI and TH.
mf_getfmtname.usage: %put %mf_getfmtname(8.); %put %mf_getfmtname($4.); %put %mf_getfmtname(comma14.10); Returns: > W > $CHAR > COMMA Note that system defaults are inferred from the values provided.
mf_getfmtname.outputs: The name (without width or decimal) of the format.
mf_getfmtname.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.fmt: The fully defined format. If left blank, nothing is returned.

mf_getkeyvalue.name: mf_getkeyvalue
mf_getkeyvalue.description: retrieves a key value pair from a control dataset
mf_getkeyvalue.descriptionMore: By default, control dataset is work.mp_setkeyvalue.  Usage: %mp_setkeyvalue(someindex,22,type=N) %put %mf_getkeyvalue(someindex)
mf_getkeyvalue.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.key: Provide a key on which to perform the lookup
parameters.libds=: define the target table which holds the parameters

mf_getplatform.name: mf_getplatform
mf_getplatform.descriptionMore: Enables platform specific variables to be returned %put %mf_getplatform(); returns one of: @li SASMETA @li SASVIYA @li SASJS @li BASESAS
mf_getplatform.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.switch: the param for which to return a platform specific variable

mf_getquotedstr.name: mf_getquotedstr
mf_getquotedstr.description: Adds custom quotes / delimiters to a  delimited string
mf_getquotedstr.descriptionMore: Can be used in open code, eg as follows: %put %mf_getquotedstr(blah   blah  blah); which returns: > 'blah','blah','blah' Alternatively: %put %mf_getquotedstr(these words are double quoted,quote=D) for: > "these","words","are","double","quoted"
mf_getquotedstr.outputs: output returns a string with the newly quoted / delimited output.
mf_getquotedstr.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.in_str: The unquoted, spaced delimited string to transform
parameters.dlm=: (,) The delimeter to be applied to the output (default comma)
parameters.indlm=: ( ) The delimeter used for the input (default is space)
parameters.quote=: (S) The quote mark to apply (S=Single, D=Double, N=None). If any other value than uppercase S or D is supplied, then that value will be used as the quoting character.

mf_getschema.name: mf_getschema
mf_getschema.description: Returns the database schema of a SAS library
mf_getschema.outputs: output returns the library schema for the FIRST library encountered @warning will only return the FIRST library schema - for concatenated libraries, with different schemas, inconsistent results may be encountered.
mf_getschema.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.libref: Library reference (also accepts a 2 level libds ref).

mf_getuniquefileref.name: mf_getuniquefileref
mf_getuniquefileref.description: Assigns and returns an unused fileref
mf_getuniquefileref.descriptionMore: Using the native approach for assigning filerefs fails as some procedures (such as proc http) do not recognise the temporary names (starting with a hash), returning a message such as: > ERROR 22-322: Expecting a name. This macro works by attempting a random fileref (with a prefix), seeing if it is already assigned, and if not - returning the fileref. If your process can accept filerefs with the hash (#) prefix, then set `prefix=0` to revert to the native approach - which is significantly faster when there are a lot of filerefs in a session. Use as follows: %let fileref1=%mf_getuniquefileref(); %let fileref2=%mf_getuniquefileref(prefix=0); %put &fileref1 &fileref2; which returns filerefs similar to: > _7432233 #LN00070
mf_getuniquefileref.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.prefix=: (_) first part of fileref. Remember that filerefs can only be 8 characters, so a 7 letter prefix would mean `maxtries` should be 10. if using zero (0) as the prefix, a native assignment is used.
parameters.maxtries=: (1000) the last part of the libref. Must be an integer.
parameters.lrecl=: (32767) Provide a default lrecl with which to initialise the generated fileref.

mf_getuniquelibref.name: mf_getuniquelibref
mf_getuniquelibref.descriptionMore: Use as follows: libname mclib0 (work); libname mclib1 (work); libname mclib2 (work); %let libref=%mf_getuniquelibref(); %put &=libref; which returns: > mclib3 A blank value is returned if no usable libname is determined.
mf_getuniquelibref.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.prefix=: (mclib) first part of the returned libref. As librefs can be as long as 8 characters, a maximum length of 7 characters is premitted for this prefix.
parameters.maxtries=: Deprecated parameter. Remains here to ensure a non-breaking change.  Will be removed in v5.

mf_getuniquename.name: mf_getuniquename
mf_getuniquename.description: Returns a shortened (32 char) GUID as a valid SAS name
mf_getuniquename.descriptionMore: Use as follows: %let myds=%mf_getuniquename(); %put &=myds; which returns: > MCc59c750610321d4c8bf75faadbcd22
mf_getuniquename.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.prefix=: set a prefix for the new name

mf_getuser.name: mf_getuser
mf_getuser.description: Returns a userid according to session context
mf_getuser.descriptionMore: In a workspace session, a user is generally represented by &sysuserid or SYS_COMPUTE_SESSION_OWNER if it exists. In a Stored Process session, &sysuserid resolves to a system account (default=sassrv) and instead there are several metadata username variables to choose from (_metauser, _metaperson ,_username, _secureusername). The OS account is represented by _secureusername whilst the metadata account is under _metaperson. %let user= %mf_getUser(); %put &user;
mf_getuser.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.type: - do not use, may be deprecated in a future release

mf_getvalue.name: mf_getvalue
mf_getvalue.description: Retrieves a value from a dataset.  If no filter supplied, then first record is used.
mf_getvalue.descriptionMore: Be sure to %quote() your where clause. Example usage: %put %mf_getvalue(sashelp.class,name,filter=%quote(age=15)); %put %mf_getvalue(sashelp.class,name);
mf_getvalue.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.variable: the variable which contains the value to return.

mf_getvarcount.name: mf_getvarcount
mf_getvarcount.description: Returns number of variables in a dataset
mf_getvarcount.descriptionMore: Useful to identify those renagade datasets that have no columns! Can also be used to count for numeric, or character columns %put Number of Variables=%mf_getvarcount(sashelp.class); %put Character Variables=%mf_getvarcount(sashelp.class,typefilter=C); %put Numeric Variables = %mf_getvarcount(sashelp.class,typefilter=N); returns: > Number of Variables=4
mf_getvarcount.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.typefilter=: (A) Filter for certain types of column.  Valid values: @li A Count All columns @li C Count Character columns only @li N Count Numeric columns only

mf_getvarformat.name: mf_getvarformat
mf_getvarformat.description: Returns the format of a variable
mf_getvarformat.descriptionMore: Uses varfmt function to identify the format of a particular variable.
mf_getvarformat.usage: data test; format str1 $1.  num1 datetime19.; str2='hello mum!'; num2=666; stop; run; %put %mf_getVarFormat(test,str1); %put %mf_getVarFormat(work.test,num1); %put %mf_getVarFormat(test,str2,force=1); %put %mf_getVarFormat(work.test,num2,force=1); %put %mf_getVarFormat(test,renegade); returns: $1. DATETIME19. $10. 8. NOTE: Variable renegade does not exist in test
mf_getvarformat.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.var: Variable name for which a format should be returned
parameters.force=(0): Set to 1 to supply a default if the variable has no format

mf_getvarlen.name: mf_getvarlen
mf_getvarlen.description: Returns the length of a variable
mf_getvarlen.descriptionMore: Uses varlen function to identify the length of a particular variable.
mf_getvarlen.usage: data test; format str $1.  num datetime19.; stop; run; %put %mf_getVarLen(test,str); %put %mf_getVarLen(work.test,num); %put %mf_getVarLen(test,renegade); returns: 1 8 NOTE: Variable renegade does not exist in test
mf_getvarlen.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.var: Variable name for which a length should be returned

mf_getvarlist.name: mf_getvarlist
mf_getvarlist.description: Returns dataset variable list direct from header
mf_getvarlist.descriptionMore: WAY faster than dictionary tables or sas views, and can also be called in macro logic (is pure macro). Can be used in open code, eg as follows: %put List of Variables=%mf_getvarlist(sashelp.class); returns: > List of Variables=Name Sex Age Height Weight For a seperated list of column values: %put %mf_getvarlist(sashelp.class,dlm=%str(,),quote=double); returns: > "Name","Sex","Age","Height","Weight"
mf_getvarlist.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.dlm=: ( ) Provide a delimiter (eg comma or space) to separate the variables
parameters.quote=: (none) use either DOUBLE or SINGLE to quote the results
parameters.typefilter=: (A) Filter for certain types of column.  Valid values: @li A Return All columns @li C Return Character columns @li N Return Numeric columns

mf_getvarnum.name: mf_getvarnum
mf_getvarnum.description: Returns the position of a variable in dataset (varnum attribute).
mf_getvarnum.descriptionMore: Uses varnum function to determine position.
mf_getvarnum.usage: data work.test; format str $1.  num datetime19.; stop; run; %put %mf_getVarNum(work.test,str); %put %mf_getVarNum(work.test,num); %put %mf_getVarNum(work.test,renegade); returns: > 1 > 2 > NOTE: Variable renegade does not exist in test
mf_getvarnum.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.var: Variable name for which a position should be returned

mf_getvartype.name: mf_getvartype
mf_getvartype.description: Returns variable type - Character (C) or Numeric (N)
mf_getvartype.usage: data test; length str $1.  num 8.; stop; run; %put %mf_getvartype(test,str); %put %mf_getvartype(work.test,num);
mf_getvartype.outputs: output returns C or N depending on variable type.  If variable does not exist then a blank is returned and a note is written to the log.
mf_getvartype.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.var: the variable name to be checked

mf_getxengine.name: mf_getxengine
mf_getxengine.description: Returns the engine type of a SAS fileref
mf_getxengine.descriptionMore: Queries sashelp.vextfl to get the xengine value.
mf_getxengine.outputs: The XENGINE value in sashelp.vextfl or 0 if not found.
mf_getxengine.author: Allan Bowe

mf_increment.name: mf_increment
mf_increment.descriptionMore: Useful outside of do-loops - will increment a macro variable every time it is called. Example: %let cnt=1; %put We have run %mf_increment(cnt) lines; %put Now we have run %mf_increment(cnt) lines; %put There are %mf_increment(cnt) lines in total;

parameters.MACRO_NAME: the name of the macro variable to increment
parameters.ITER=:
The amount to add or subtract to the macro

Related Files

@li mf_increment.test.sas

mf_isblank.name: mf_isblank
mf_isblank.description: Checks whether a macro variable is empty (blank)

mf_isdir.name: mf_isdir
mf_isdir.description: Checks whether a path is a valid directory
mf_isdir.usage: %let isdir=%mf_isdir(/tmp); With thanks and full credit to Andrea Defronzo - https://www.linkedin.com/in/andrea-defronzo-b1a47460
mf_isdir.outputs: output returns 1 if path is a directory, 0 if it is not

parameters.path: full path of the file/directory to be checked

mf_isint.name: mf_isint
mf_isint.description: Returns 1 if the variable contains only digits 0-9, else 0
mf_isint.descriptionMore: Note that numerics containing any punctuation (including decimals or exponents) will be flagged zero. If you'd like support for this, then do raise an issue (or even better, a pull request!)

mf_islibds.name: mf_islibds
mf_islibds.description: Checks whether a string follows correct library.dataset format
mf_islibds.descriptionMore: Many macros in the core library accept a library.dataset parameter referred to as 'libds'.  This macro validates the structure of that parameter, eg: @li 8 character libref? @li 32 character dataset? @li contains a period? It does NOT check whether the dataset exists, or if the library is assigned.
mf_islibds.outputs: output Returns 1 if libds is valid, 0 if it is not

mf_loc.name: mf_loc
mf_loc.description: Returns physical location of various SAS items
mf_loc.descriptionMore: Returns location of the PlatformObjectFramework tools
mf_loc.author: Allan Bowe

mf_mkdir.name: mf_mkdir
mf_mkdir.description: Creates a directory, including any intermediate directories
mf_mkdir.descriptionMore: Works on windows and unix environments via dcreate function.

mf_mval.name: mf_mval
mf_mval.description: Returns a macro variable value if the variable exists
mf_mval.descriptionMore: Use this macro to avoid repetitive use of `%if %symexist(MACVAR) %then` type logic.
mf_mval.author: Allan Bowe

mf_nobs.name: mf_nobs
mf_nobs.descriptionMore: Beware - will not work on external database tables! Is just a convenience macro for calling %mf_getattrn(). %put Number of observations=%mf_nobs(sashelp.class);
mf_nobs.outputs: output returns result of the attrn value supplied, or log message if err.
mf_nobs.author: Allan Bowe

mf_trimstr.name: mf_trimstr
mf_trimstr.description: Removes character(s) from the end, if they exist
mf_trimstr.descriptionMore: If the designated characters exist at the end of the string, they are removed %put %mf_trimstr(/blah/,/); * /blah; %put %mf_trimstr(/blah/,h); * /blah/; %put %mf_trimstr(/blah/,h/);* /bla;
mf_trimstr.outputs: output returns result with the value of `trimstr` removed from the end
mf_trimstr.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.trimstr: The string to be removed from the end of `basestr`, if it exists

mf_uid.name: mf_uid
mf_uid.description: Creates a unique ID based on system time in friendly format
mf_uid.author: Allan Bowe

mf_verifymacvars.name: mf_verifymacvars
mf_verifymacvars.description: Checks if a set of macro variables exist AND contain values.
mf_verifymacvars.descriptionMore: Writes ERROR to log if abortType is SOFT, else will call %mf_abort.
mf_verifymacvars.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.verifyvars: Space separated list of macro variable names
parameters.makeupcase=: (NO) Set to YES to convert all variable VALUES to uppercase.
parameters.mAbort=: (SOFT) Abort Type.  When SOFT, simply writes an err message to the log. Set to any other value to call mf_abort (which can be configured to abort in various fashions according to context). @warning will not be able to verify the following variables due to naming clash! - verifyVars - verifyVar - verifyIterator - makeUpcase

mf_wordsinstr1andstr2.name: mf_wordsinstr1andstr2
mf_wordsinstr1andstr2.description: Returns words that are in both string 1 and string 2
mf_wordsinstr1andstr2.descriptionMore: Compares two space separated strings and returns the words that are in both.
mf_wordsinstr1andstr2.usage: %put %mf_wordsInStr1andStr2( Str1=blah sss blaaah brah bram boo ,Str2=   blah blaaah brah ssss ); returns: > blah blaaah brah
mf_wordsinstr1andstr2.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.str2=: used to compare with the extract string @warning CASE SENSITIVE!

mf_wordsinstr1butnotstr2.name: mf_wordsinstr1butnotstr2
mf_wordsinstr1butnotstr2.description: Returns words that are in string 1 but not in string 2
mf_wordsinstr1butnotstr2.descriptionMore: Compares two space separated strings and returns the words that are in the first but not in the second. Note - case sensitive!
mf_wordsinstr1butnotstr2.usage: %let x= %mf_wordsInStr1ButNotStr2( Str1=blah sss blaaah brah bram boo ,Str2=   blah blaaah brah ssss ); returns: > sss bram boo
mf_wordsinstr1butnotstr2.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.str2=: used to compare with the extract string

mf_writefile.name: mf_writefile
mf_writefile.description: Creates a text file using pure macro
mf_writefile.descriptionMore: Creates a text file of up to 10 lines.  If further lines are desired, feel free to [create an issue]( https://github.com/sasjs/core/issues/new), or make a pull request! The use of PARMBUFF was considered for this macro, but it would have made things problematic for writing lines containing commas.
mf_writefile.usage: %mf_writefile(&sasjswork/myfile.txt,l1=some content,l2=more content) data _null_; infile "&sasjswork/myfile.txt"; input; list; run;
mf_writefile.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.fpath: Full path to file to be created or appended to
parameters.mode=: (O) Available options are A or O as follows: @li A APPEND mode, writes new records after the current end of the file. @li O OUTPUT mode, writes new records from the beginning of the file.

mfv_existfile.name: mfv_existfile
mfv_existfile.description: Checks whether a file exists in SAS Drive (only works in Viya)
mfv_existfile.descriptionMore: Returns 1 if the file exists, and 0 if it doesn't.  Works by attempting to assign a fileref with the filesrvc engine.  If not found, the syscc is automatically set to a non zero value - so in this case it is reset. To avoid hiding issues, there is therefore a test at the start to ensure the syscc is zero.
mfv_existfile.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.filepath: The full path to the file on SAS drive (eg /Public/myfile.txt)

mfv_existfolder.name: mfv_existfolder
mfv_existfolder.description: Checks whether a folder exists in SAS Drive (only works in Viya)
mfv_existfolder.descriptionMore: Returns 1 if the folder exists, and 0 if it doesn't.  Works by attempting to assign a fileref with the filesrvc engine.  If not found, the syscc is automatically set to a non zero value - so in this case it is reset. To avoid hiding issues, there is therefore a test at the start to ensure the syscc is zero.
mfv_existfolder.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.path: The path to the folder on SAS drive

mfv_existsashdat.name: mfv_existsashdat
mfv_existsashdat.description: Checks whether a CAS sashdat dataset exists in persistent storage. (only works in Viya)
mfv_existsashdat.descriptionMore: Can be used in open code, eg as follows: %if %mfv_existsashdat(libds=casuser.sometable) %then %put  yes it does!; The function uses `dosubl()` to run the `table.fileinfo` action, for the specified library, filtering for `*.sashdat` tables.  The results are stored in a WORK table (&outprefix._&lib). If that table already exists, it is queried instead, to avoid the dosubl() performance hit. To force a rescan, just use a new `&outprefix` value, or delete the table(s) before running the function.
mfv_existsashdat.author: Mathieu Blauw

parameters.outprefix=: (work.mfv_existsashdat) Used to store the current HDATA tables to improve subsequent query performance.  This reference is a prefix and is converted to `&prefix._{libref}`

mm_createlibrary.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.libname=: Library name (as displayed to user, 256 chars). Duplicates are not created (case sensitive).
parameters.libref=: Library libref (8 chars).  Duplicate librefs are not created, HOWEVER- the check is not case sensitive - if *libref* exists, *LIBREF* will still be created.   Librefs created will always be uppercased.
parameters.tree=: The metadata folder uri, or the metadata path, in which to create the library.

If more than one uri found with that path, then the first one will be used. If no URI is found, a new directory object will be created.  The physical path will also be created, if it doesn't exist.
parameters.mDebug=: set to 1 to show debug messages in the log

mm_createstp.name: mm_createstp
mm_createstp.description: Create a type 1 Stored Process (9.2 compatible) (interface with the metadata server)
mm_createstp.descriptionMore: This macro creates a Type 1 stored process, and also the necessary PromptGroup / File / TextStore objects.  It requires the location (or uri) for the App Server / Directory / Folder (Tree) objects. To upgrade this macro to work with type 2 (which can embed SAS code and is compabitible with SAS from 9.3 onwards) then the UsageVersion should change to 2000000 and the TextStore object updated.  The ComputeServer reference will also be to ServerContext rather than LogicalServer. This macro is idempotent - if you run it twice, it will only create an STP once. Usage (type 1 STP): %mm_createstp(stpname=MyNewSTP ,filename=mySpecialProgram.sas ,directory=SASEnvironment/SASCode/STPs ,tree=/User Folders/sasdemo ,outds=work.uris) If you wish to remove the new STP you can do so by running: data _null_; set work.uris; rc1 = METADATA_DELOBJ(texturi); rc2 = METADATA_DELOBJ(prompturi); rc3 = METADATA_DELOBJ(fileuri); rc4 = METADATA_DELOBJ(stpuri); putlog (_all_)(=); run; Usage (type 2 STP): %mm_createstp(stpname=MyNewType2STP ,filename=mySpecialProgram.sas ,directory=SASEnvironment/SASCode/STPs ,tree=/User Folders/sasdemo ,Server=SASApp ,stptype=2)
mm_createstp.outputs: outds  dataset containing the following columns: - stpuri - prompturi - fileuri - texturi
mm_createstp.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.stpname=: Stored Process name.  Avoid spaces - testing has shown that the check to avoid creating multiple STPs in the same folder with the same name does not work when the name contains spaces.
parameters.filename=: the name of the .sas program to run
parameters.directory=: The directory uri, or the actual path to the sas program (no trailing slash).  If more than uri is found with that path, then the first one will be used.
parameters.tree=: The metadata folder uri, or the metadata path, in which to create the STP.
parameters.server=: The server which will run the STP.  Server name or uri is fine.
parameters.outds=: The two level name of the output dataset.  Will contain all the meta uris. Defaults to work.mm_createstp.
parameters.mDebug=: set to 1 to show debug messages in the log
parameters.stptype=: Default is 1 (STP code saved on filesystem).  Set to 2 if source code is to be saved in metadata (9.3 and above feature).
parameters.minify=: set to YES to strip comments / blank lines etc
parameters.frefin=: fileref to use (enables change if there is a conflict).  The filerefs are left open, to enable inspection after running the macro (or importing into an xmlmap if needed).
parameters.frefout=: fileref to use (enables change if there is a conflict)
parameters.repo=: ServerContext is tied to a repo, if you are not using the foundation repo then select a different one here

mm_createwebservice.name: mm_createwebservice
mm_createwebservice.description: Create a Web Ready Stored Process (interface with the metadata server)
mm_createwebservice.descriptionMore: This macro creates a Type 2 Stored Process with the mm_webout macro (and dependencies) included as pre-code.
mm_createwebservice.usage: %* compile macros ; filename mc url "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sasjs/core/main/all.sas"; %inc mc; %* parmcards lets us write to a text file from open code ; filename ft15f001 temp; parmcards4; %webout(FETCH) %* do some sas, any inputs are now already WORK tables; data example1 example2; set sashelp.class; run; %* send data back; %webout(OPEN) %webout(ARR,example1) * Array format, fast, suitable for large tables ; %webout(OBJ,example2) * Object format, easier to work with ; %webout(CLOSE) ;;;; %mm_createwebservice(path=/Public/app/common,name=appInit,code=ft15f001) For more examples of using these web services with the SASjs Adapter, see: https://github.com/sasjs/adapter#readme
mm_createwebservice.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.path=: The full path (in SAS Metadata) where the service will be created
parameters.name=: Stored Process name.  Avoid spaces - testing has shown that the check to avoid creating multiple STPs in the same folder with the same name does not work when the name contains spaces.
parameters.precode=: Space separated list of filerefs, pointing to the code that needs to be attached to the beginning of the service (optional)
parameters.code=: (ft15f001) Space seperated fileref(s) of the actual code to be added
parameters.server=: (SASApp) The server which will run the STP.  Server name or uri is fine.
parameters.mDebug=: (0) set to 1 to show debug messages in the log
parameters.replace=: (YES) select NO to avoid replacing an existing service in that location
parameters.adapter=: (sasjs) the macro uses the sasjs adapter by default.  To use another adapter, add a (different) fileref here.

parameters.inds=: an optional input dataset to augment with treepath & treeuri
parameters.mDebug=: set to 1 to show debug messages in the log

mm_gettypes.name: mm_gettypes
mm_gettypes.description: Creates a dataset with all metadata types (interface with the metadata server)

mm_gettypes.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.outds: the dataset to create that contains the list of types

mm_getusers.name: mm_getusers
mm_getusers.description: Creates a table containing a list of all users (interface with the metadata server)
mm_getusers.descriptionMore: Only shows a limited number of attributes as some sites will have a LOT of users.
mm_getusers.usage: %mm_getusers() Optionally, filter for a user (useful to get the uri): %mm_getusers(user=&_metaperson)
mm_getusers.outputs: outds  dataset containing all users, with the following columns: - uri - name
mm_getusers.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.outds: the dataset to create that contains the list of libraries

mm_getwebappsrvprops.name: mm_getwebappsrvprops
mm_getwebappsrvprops.description: Retrieves properties of the SAS web app server (interface with the metadata server)

mm_getwebappsrvprops.outputs: outds  dataset containing all properties @warning The following filenames are created and then de-assigned: filename __in clear; filename __out clear; libname __shake clear;
mm_getwebappsrvprops.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.outds: the dataset to create that contains the list of properties

mm_spkexport.name: mm_spkexport
mm_spkexport.description: Creates an batch spk export command (interface with the metadata server)
mm_spkexport.descriptionMore: Creates a script that will export everything in a metadata folder to a specified location. If you have XCMD enabled, then you can use mmx_spkexport (which performs the actual export) Note - the batch tools require a username and password.  For security, these are expected to have been provided in a protected directory.
mm_spkexport.usage: %* import the macros (or make them available some other way); filename mc url "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sasjs/core/main/all.sas"; %inc mc; %* create sample text file as input to the macro; filename tmp temp; data _null_; file tmp; put '%let mmxuser="sasdemo";'; put '%let mmxpass="Mars321";'; run; filename myref "%sysfunc(pathname(work))/mmxexport.sh" permission='A::u::rwx,A::g::r-x,A::o::---'; %mm_spkexport(metaloc=%str(/my/meta/loc) ,outref=myref ,secureref=tmp ,cmdoutloc=%str(/tmp) ) Alternatively, call without inputs to create a function style output filename myref "/tmp/mmscript.sh" permission='A::u::rwx,A::g::r-x,A::o::---'; %mm_spkexport(metaloc=%str(/my/meta/loc) outref=myref ,cmdoutloc=%str(/tmp) ,cmdoutname=mmx ) You can then navigate and execute as follows: cd /tmp ./mmscript.sh "myuser" "mypass"
mm_spkexport.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.secureref=: fileref containing the username / password (should point to a file in a secure location). Leave blank to substitute $bash vars.
parameters.excludevars=: (0) A space seperated list of macro variable names, each of which contains a value that should be used to filter the output objects.
parameters.outref=: fileref to which to write the command
parameters.cmdoutloc=: (%sysfunc(pathname(work))) The directory to which the command will write the SPK
parameters.cmdoutname=: (mmxport) The name of the spk / log files to create (will be identical just with .spk or .log extension)

mm_tree.name: mm_tree
mm_tree.description: Returns all folders / subfolder content for a particular root (interface with the metadata server)
mm_tree.descriptionMore: Shows all members and SubTrees for a particular root. Model: metauri char(64), name char(256) format=$256. informat=$256. label='name', path char(1024), publictype char(32), MetadataUpdated char(32), MetadataCreated char(32)
mm_tree.usage: %* load macros; filename mc url "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sasjs/core/main/all.sas"; %inc mc; %* export everything; %mm_tree(root= ,outds=iwantthisdataset) %* export everything in a specific folder; %mm_tree(root=%str(/my/folder) ,outds=stuff) %* export only folders; %mm_tree(root=%str(/my/folder) ,types=Folder ,outds=stuf) %* show only exportable content; %mm_tree(root=%str(/) ,types=EXPORTABLE ,outds=exportable) %* with specific types; %mm_tree(root=%str(/my/folder) ,types= DeployedJob ExternalFile Folder Folder.SecuredData GeneratedTransform InformationMap.Relational Job Library Prompt StoredProcess Table ,outds=morestuff)
mm_tree.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.root=: the parent folder under which to return all contents
parameters.outds=: the dataset to create that contains the list of directories
parameters.types=: Space-seperated, unquoted list of types for filtering the output.  Special types: * ALl - return all types (the default) * EXPORTABLE - return only the content types that can be exported in an SPK

mm_updateappextension.name: mm_updateappextension
mm_updateappextension.description: Add or update an extension to an application component (interface with the metadata server)
mm_updateappextension.descriptionMore: A SAS Application (SoftwareComponent) is a great place to store app specific parameters.  There are two main places those params can be stored: 1) Configuration, and 2) Extensions.  The second location will enable end users to modify parameters even if they don't have the Configuration Manager plugin in SMC.  This macro can be used after creating an application with the mm_createapplication.sas macro.  If a parameter with the same name exists, it is updated.  If it does not, it is created.
mm_updateappextension.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.frefin=: change default inref if it clashes with an existing one
parameters.frefout=: change default outref if it clashes with an existing one
parameters.mDebug=: set to 1 to show debug messages in the log

mm_updatedocument.name: mm_updatedocument
mm_updatedocument.description: Update the TextStore in a Document with the same name (interface with the metadata server)

mm_updatedocument.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.text=a: source file containing the text to be added
parameters.frefin=: change default inref if it clashes with an existing one

parameters.mDebug=: set to 1 to show debug messages in the log

mm_updatestpservertype.name: mm_updatestpservertype
mm_updatestpservertype.description: Updates a type 2 stored process to run on STP or WKS context (interface with the metadata server)
mm_updatestpservertype.descriptionMore: Only works on Type 2 (9.3 compatible) STPs
mm_updatestpservertype.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.target=: full path to the STP being deleted
parameters.type=: Either WKS or STP depending on whether Workspace or Stored Process type required

mm_updatestpsourcecode.name: mm_updatestpsourcecode
mm_updatestpsourcecode.description: Update the source code of a type 2 STP (interface with the metadata server)
mm_updatestpsourcecode.descriptionMore: Uploads the contents of a text file or fileref to an existing type 2 STP.  A type 2 STP has its source code saved in metadata.

parameters.stp=: the BIP Tree folder path plus Stored Process Name
parameters.stpcode=: the source file (or fileref) containing the SAS code to load into the stp.  For multiple files, they should simply be concatenated first.
parameters.minify=: set to YES in order to strip comments, blank lines, and CRLFs.
parameters.mDebug=: set to 1 to show debug messages in the log

mm_webout.name: mm_webout
mm_webout.description: Send data to/from SAS Stored Processes (interface with the metadata server)
mm_webout.descriptionMore: This macro should be added to the start of each Stored Process, **immediately** followed by a call to: %mm_webout(FETCH) This will read all the input data and create same-named SAS datasets in the WORK library.  You can then insert your code, and send data back using the following syntax: data some datasets; * make some data ; retain some columns; run; %mm_webout(OPEN) %mm_webout(ARR,some)  * Array format, fast, suitable for large tables ; %mm_webout(OBJ,datasets) * Object format, easier to work with ; Finally, wrap everything up send some helpful system variables too %mm_webout(CLOSE)
mm_webout.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.action: Either FETCH, OPEN, ARR, OBJ or CLOSE
parameters.ds: The dataset to send back to the frontend
parameters.dslabel=: Value to use instead of table name for sending to JSON
parameters.fmt=: (N) Setting Y converts all vars to their formatted values
parameters.fref=: (_webout) The fileref to which to write the JSON
parameters.missing=: (NULL) Special numeric missing values can be sent as NULL (eg `null`) or as STRING values (eg `".a"` or `".b"`)
parameters.showmeta=: (N) Set to Y to output metadata alongside each table, such as the column formats and types.  The metadata is contained inside an object with the same name as the table but prefixed with a dollar sign - ie, `,"$tablename":{"formats":{"col1":"$CHAR1"},"types":{"COL1":"C"}}`
parameters.workobs=: (0) When set to a positive integer, will create a new output object (WORK) which contains this number of observations from all tables in the WORK library.
parameters.maxobs=: (MAX) Provide an integer to limit the number of input rows that should be converted to output JSON

mmx_createmetafolder.name: mmx_createmetafolder
mmx_createmetafolder.description: Creates a metadata folder (interface with the metadata server)
mmx_createmetafolder.descriptionMore: Creates a metadata folder using the batch tools
mmx_createmetafolder.author: Allan Bowe

mmx_deletemetafolder.name: mmx_deletemetafolder
mmx_deletemetafolder.description: Deletes a metadata folder (interface with the metadata server)
mmx_deletemetafolder.descriptionMore: Deletes a metadata folder (and contents) using the batch tools, as documented here: https://documentation.sas.com/?docsetId=bisag&docsetTarget=p0zqp8fmgs4o0kn1tt7j8ho829fv.htm&docsetVersion=9.4&locale=en
mmx_deletemetafolder.author: Allan Bowe

mmx_spkexport.name: mmx_spkexport
mmx_spkexport.description: Exports everything in a particular metadata folder (interface with the metadata server)
mmx_spkexport.descriptionMore: Will export everything in a metadata folder to a specified location. Note - the batch tools require a username and password.  For security, these are expected to have been provided in a protected directory.
mmx_spkexport.usage: %* import the macros (or make them available some other way); filename mc url "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sasjs/core/main/all.sas"; %inc mc; %* create sample text file as input to the macro; filename tmp temp; data _null_; file tmp; put '%let mmxuser=sasdemo;'; put '%let mmxpass=Mars321'; run; filename outref "%sysfunc(pathname(work))"; %mmx_spkexport( metaloc=%str(/30.Projects/3001.Internal/300115.DataController/dc1) ,secureref=tmp ,outspkpath=%str(/tmp) )
mmx_spkexport.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.secureref=: fileref containing the username / password (should point to a file in a secure location)
parameters.outspkname=: name of the spk to be created (default is mmxport).
parameters.outspkpath=: directory in which to create the SPK.  Default is WORK.

mp_abort.name: mp_abort
mp_abort.descriptionMore: Configures an abort mechanism according to site specific policies or the particulars of an environment.  For instance, can stream custom results back to the client in an STP Web App context, or completely stop in the case of a batch run.  For STP sessions The method used varies according to the context.  Important points: @li should not use endsas or abort cancel in 9.4m3 WIN environments as this can cause hung multibridge sessions and result in a frozen STP server @li The use of endsas in 9.4m6+ windows environments for POST requests to the STP server can result in an empty response body @li should not use endsas in viya 3.5 as this destroys the session and cannot fetch results (although both mv_getjoblog.sas and the @sasjs/adapter will recognise this and fetch the log of the parent session instead) @li STP environments must finish cleanly to avoid the log being sent to _webout.  To assist with this, we also run stpsrvset('program error', 0) and set SYSCC=0. Where possible, we take a unique "soft abort" approach - we open a macro but don't close it!  This works everywhere EXCEPT inside a \%include inside a macro.  For that, we recommend you use mp_include.sas to perform the include, and then call \%mp_abort(mode=INCLUDE) from the source program (ie, OUTSIDE of the top-parent macro). The soft abort has become ineffective in 9.4m6 WINDOWS environments.  We are currently investigating approaches to deal with this.
mp_abort.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.mac=: (mp_abort.sas) To contain the name of the calling macro. Do not use &sysmacroname as this will always resolve to MP_ABORT.
parameters.iftrue=: (1=1) Supply a condition for which the macro should be executed
parameters.errds=: (work.mp_abort_errds) There is no clean way to end a process within a %include called within a macro.  Furthermore, there is no way to test if a macro is called within a %include.  To handle this particular scenario, the %include should be switched for the mp_include.sas macro. This provides an indicator that we are running a macro within a %include (`_SYSINCLUDEFILEDEVICE`) and allows us to provide a dataset with the abort values (msg, mac). We can then run an abort cancel FILE to stop the include running, and pass the dataset back to the calling program to run a regular %mp_abort(). The dataset will contain the following fields: @li iftrue (1=1) @li msg (the message) @li mac (the mac param)
parameters.mode=: (REGULAR) If mode=INCLUDE then the &errds dataset is checked for an abort status. Valid values: @li REGULAR (default) @li INCLUDE

mp_appendfile.name: mp_appendfile
mp_appendfile.descriptionMore: Will append one more more `appendrefs` (filerefs) to a `baseref`. Uses a binary mechanism, so will work with any file type.  For that reason - use with care!   And supply your own trailing carriage returns in each file..
mp_appendfile.usage: filename tmp1 temp; filename tmp2 temp; filename tmp3 temp; data _null_; file tmp1; put 'base file'; data _null_; file tmp2; put 'append1'; data _null_; file tmp3; put 'append2'; run; %mp_appendfile(baseref=tmp1, appendrefs=tmp2 tmp3)
mp_appendfile.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.baseref=: Fileref of the base file (should exist)
parameters.appendrefs=: One or more filerefs to be appended to the base fileref.  Space separated.

mp_applyformats.name: mp_applyformats
mp_applyformats.description: Apply a set of formats to a table
mp_applyformats.descriptionMore: Applies a set of formats to the metadata of one or more SAS datasets. Can be used to migrate formats from one table to another.  The input table must contain the following columns: @li lib - the libref of the table to be updated @li ds - the dataset to be updated @li var - the variable to be updated @li fmt - the format to apply.  Missing or default ($CHAR, 8.) formats are ignored. The macro will abort in the following scenarios: @li Libref not assigned @li Dataset does not exist @li Input table contains null or invalid values Example usage: data work.example; set sashelp.prdsale; format _all_ clear; run; %mp_getcols(sashelp.prdsale,outds=work.cols) data work.cols2; set work.cols; lib='WORK'; ds='EXAMPLE'; var=name; fmt=format; keep lib ds var fmt; run; %mp_applyformats(work.cols2)
mp_applyformats.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.inds: The input dataset containing the formats to apply (and where to apply them).  Example structure: |LIB:$8.|DS:$32.|VAR:$32.|FMT:$49.| |---|---|---|---| |`WORK `|`EXAMPLE `|`ACTUAL `|`DOLLAR12.2 `| |`WORK `|`EXAMPLE `|`COUNTRY `|`$CHAR10. `| |`WORK `|`EXAMPLE `|`DIVISION `|`$CHAR10. `| |`WORK `|`EXAMPLE `|`MONTH `|`MONNAME3. `| |`WORK `|`EXAMPLE `|`PREDICT `|`DOLLAR12.2 `| |`WORK `|`EXAMPLE `|`PRODTYPE `|`$CHAR10. `| |`WORK `|`EXAMPLE `|`PRODUCT `|`$CHAR10. `| |`WORK `|`EXAMPLE `|`QUARTER `|`8. `| |`WORK `|`EXAMPLE `|`REGION `|`$CHAR10. `| |`WORK `|`EXAMPLE `|`YEAR `|`8. `|
parameters.errds=: (0) Provide a libds reference here to export the error messages to a table.  In this case, they will not be printed to the log.

mp_assert.name: mp_assert
mp_assert.descriptionMore: Useful in the context of writing sasjs tests.  The results of the test are _appended_ to the &outds. table. Example usage: %mp_assert(iftrue=(1=1), desc=Obviously true ) %mp_assert(iftrue=(1=0), desc=Will fail )
mp_assert.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.iftrue=: (1=1) A condition where, if true, the test is a PASS. Else, the test is a fail.
parameters.desc=: (Testing observations) The user provided test description
parameters.outds=: (work.test_results) The output dataset to contain the results.  If it does not exist, it will be created, with the following format: |TEST_DESCRIPTION:$256|TEST_RESULT:$4|TEST_COMMENTS:$256| |---|---|---| |User Provided description|PASS|Column &inds contained ALL columns|

mp_assertcols.name: mp_assertcols
mp_assertcols.description: Asserts the existence (or not) of columns
mp_assertcols.descriptionMore: Useful in the context of writing sasjs tests.  The results of the test are _appended_ to the &outds. table. Example usage: %mp_assertcols(sashelp.class, cols=name age sex, test=ALL, desc=check all columns exist ) %mp_assertcols(sashelp.class, cols=a b c, test=NONE ) %mp_assertcols(sashelp.class, cols=age depth, test=ANY )
mp_assertcols.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.inds: The input library.dataset to test for values
parameters.cols=: The list of columns to check for
parameters.desc=: (Testing observations) The user provided test description
parameters.test=: (ALL) The test to apply.  Valid values are: @li ALL - Test is a PASS if ALL columns exist in &inds @li ANY - Test is a PASS if ANY of the columns exist in &inds @li NONE - Test is a PASS if NONE of the columns exist in &inds
parameters.outds=: (work.test_results) The output dataset to contain the results.  If it does not exist, it will be created, with the following format: |TEST_DESCRIPTION:$256|TEST_RESULT:$4|TEST_COMMENTS:$256| |---|---|---| |User Provided description|PASS|Column &inds contained ALL columns|

mp_assertcolvals.name: mp_assertcolvals
mp_assertcolvals.description: Asserts the values in a column
mp_assertcolvals.descriptionMore: Useful in the context of writing sasjs tests.  The results of the test are _appended_ to the &outds. table. Example usage: data work.checkds; do checkval='Jane','James','Jill'; output; end; run; %mp_assertcolvals(sashelp.class.name, checkvals=work.checkds.checkval, desc=At least one value has a match, test=ANYVAL ) data work.check; do val='M','F'; output; end; run; %mp_assertcolvals(sashelp.class.sex, checkvals=work.check.val, desc=All values have a match, test=ALLVALS )
mp_assertcolvals.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.indscol: The input library.dataset.column to test for values
parameters.checkvals=: A library.dataset.column value containing a UNIQUE list of values to be compared against the source (indscol).
parameters.desc=: (Testing observations) The user provided test description
parameters.test=: (ALLVALS) The test to apply.  Valid values are: @li ALLVALS - Test is a PASS if ALL values have a match in checkvals @li ANYVAL - Test is a PASS if at least 1 value has a match in checkvals
parameters.outds=: (work.test_results) The output dataset to contain the results.  If it does not exist, it will be created, with the following format: |TEST_DESCRIPTION:$256|TEST_RESULT:$4|TEST_COMMENTS:$256| |---|---|---| |User Provided description|PASS|Column &indscol contained ALL target vals|

mp_assertdsobs.name: mp_assertdsobs
mp_assertdsobs.description: Asserts the number of observations in a dataset
mp_assertdsobs.descriptionMore: Useful in the context of writing sasjs tests.  The results of the test are _appended_ to the &outds. table. Example usage: %mp_assertdsobs(sashelp.class) %* tests if any observations are present; %mp_assertdsobs(sashelp.class,test=ATLEAST 10) %* pass if >9 obs present; %mp_assertdsobs(sashelp.class,test=ATMOST 20) %* pass if <21 obs present;
mp_assertdsobs.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.inds: input dataset to test for presence of observations
parameters.desc=: (Testing observations) The user provided test description
parameters.test=: (HASOBS) The test to apply.  Valid values are: @li HASOBS - Test is a PASS if the input dataset has any observations @li EMPTY - Test is a PASS if input dataset is empty @li EQUALS [integer] - Test passes if row count matches the provided integer @li ATLEAST [integer] - Test passes if row count is more than or equal to the provided integer @li ATMOST [integer] - Test passes if row count is less than or equal to the provided integer
parameters.outds=: (work.test_results) The output dataset to contain the results.  If it does not exist, it will be created, with the following format: |TEST_DESCRIPTION:$256|TEST_RESULT:$4|TEST_COMMENTS:$256| |---|---|---| |User Provided description|PASS|Dataset &inds has XX obs|

mp_assertscope.name: mp_assertscope
mp_assertscope.description: Used to capture scope leakage of macro variables
mp_assertscope.descriptionMore: A common 'difficult to detect' bug in macros is where a nested macro over-writes variables in a higher level macro. This assertion takes a snapshot of the macro variables before and after a macro invocation.  Differences are captured in the `&outds` table. This makes it easy to detect whether any macro variables were modified or changed. The following variables are NOT tested (as they are known, global variables used in SASjs): @li &sasjs_prefix._FUNCTIONS Global variables are initialised in mp_init.sas - which will also trigger "strict mode" in your SAS session.  Whilst this is a default in SASjs produced apps, if you prefer not to use this mode, simply instantiate the following variable to prevent the macro from running:  `SASJS_PREFIX` Example usage: %mp_assertscope(SNAPSHOT) %let oops=I did it again; %mp_assertscope(COMPARE, desc=Checking macro variables against previous snapshot ) This macro is designed to work alongside `sasjs test` - for more information about this facility, visit [cli.sasjs.io/test](https://cli.sasjs.io/test).
mp_assertscope.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.action: (SNAPSHOT) The action to take.  Valid values: @li SNAPSHOT - take a copy of the current macro variables @li COMPARE - compare the current macro variables against previous values
parameters.scope=: (GLOBAL) The scope of the variables to be checked.  This corresponds to the values in the SCOPE column in `sashelp.vmacro`.
parameters.desc=: (Testing scope leakage) The user provided test description
parameters.ignorelist=: Provide a list of macro variable names to ignore from the comparison
parameters.[in,out]: scopeds= (work.mp_assertscope) The dataset to contain the scope snapshot
parameters.outds=: (work.test_results) The output dataset to contain the results.  If it does not exist, it will be created, with the following format: |TEST_DESCRIPTION:$256|TEST_RESULT:$4|TEST_COMMENTS:$256| |---|---|---| |User Provided description|PASS|No out of scope variables created or modified|

mp_base64copy.name: mp_base64copy
mp_base64copy.description: Convert a file to/from base64 format
mp_base64copy.descriptionMore: Creates a new version of a file either encoded or decoded using Base64.  Inspired by this post by Michael Dixon: https://support.selerity.com.au/hc/en-us/articles/223345708-Tip-SAS-and-Base64
mp_base64copy.usage: filename tmp temp; data _null_; file tmp; put 'base ik ally'; run; %mp_base64copy(inref=tmp, outref=myref, action=ENCODE) data _null_; infile myref; input; put _infile_; run; %mp_base64copy(inref=myref, outref=mynewref, action=DECODE) data _null_; infile mynewref; input; put _infile_; run;
mp_base64copy.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.inref=: Fileref of the input file (should exist)
parameters.outref=: Output fileref. If it does not exist, it is created.
parameters.action=: (ENCODE) The action to take. Valid values: @li ENCODE - Convert the file to base64 format @li DECODE - Decode the file from base64 format

mp_binarycopy.name: mp_binarycopy
mp_binarycopy.description: Copy any file using binary input / output streams
mp_binarycopy.descriptionMore: Reads in a file byte by byte and writes it back out.  Is an os-independent method to copy files.  In case of naming collision, the default filerefs can be modified. Note that if you have a new enough version of SAS, and you don't need features such as APPEND, you may be better of using the fcopy() function instead. %mp_binarycopy(inloc="/home/me/blah.txt", outref=_webout) To append to a file, use the mode option, eg: filename tmp1 temp; filename tmp2 temp; data _null_; file tmp1; put 'stacking'; run; %mp_binarycopy(inref=tmp1, outref=tmp2, mode=APPEND) %mp_binarycopy(inref=tmp1, outref=tmp2, mode=APPEND)

parameters.inloc: quoted "path/and/filename.ext" of the file to be copied
parameters.outloc: quoted "path/and/filename.ext" of the file to be created
parameters.inref: (____in) If provided, this fileref will take precedence over the `inloc` parameter
parameters.outref: (____in) If provided, this fileref will take precedence over the `outloc` parameter.  It must already exist!
parameters.mode: (CREATE) Valid values: @li CREATE - Create the file (even if it already exists) @li APPEND - Append to the file (don't overwrite)
parameters.iftrue=: (1=1) Supply a condition for which the macro should be executed

mp_chop.name: mp_chop
mp_chop.description: Splits a file of ANY SIZE by reference to a search string.
mp_chop.descriptionMore: Provide a fileref and a search string to chop off part of a file. Works by reading in the file byte by byte, then marking the beginning and end of each matched string, before finally doing the chop. Choose whether to keep the FIRST or the LAST section of the file.  Optionally, use an OFFSET to fix the precise chop point.
mp_chop.usage: %let src="%sysfunc(pathname(work))/file.txt"; %let str=Chop here!; %let out1="%sysfunc(pathname(work))/file1.txt"; %let out2="%sysfunc(pathname(work))/file2.txt"; %let out3="%sysfunc(pathname(work))/file3.txt"; %let out4="%sysfunc(pathname(work))/file4.txt"; data _null_; file &src; put "startsection&str.endsection"; run; %mp_chop(&src, matchvar=str, keep=FIRST, outfile=&out1) %mp_chop(&src, matchvar=str, keep=LAST, outfile=&out2) %mp_chop(&src, matchvar=str, keep=FIRST, matchpoint=END, outfile=&out3) %mp_chop(&src, matchvar=str, keep=LAST, matchpoint=END, outfile=&out4) filename results (&out1 &out2 &out3 &out4); data _null_; infile results; input; list; run; Results: @li `startsection` @li `Chop here!endsection` @li `startsectionChop here!` @li `endsection` For more examples, see mp_chop.test.sas
mp_chop.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.infile: The QUOTED path to the file on which to perform the chop

mp_filtercheck.name: mp_filtercheck
mp_filtercheck.description: Checks an input filter table for validity
mp_filtercheck.descriptionMore: Performs checks on the input table to ensure it arrives in the correct format.  This is necessary to prevent code injection.  Will update SYSCC to 1008 if bad records are found, and call mp_abort.sas for a graceful service exit (configurable). Used for dynamic filtering in [Data Controller for SAS®]( https://datacontroller.io).
mp_filtercheck.usage: %mp_filtercheck(work.filter,targetds=sashelp.class,outds=work.badrecords) The input table should have the following format: |GROUP_LOGIC:$3|SUBGROUP_LOGIC:$3|SUBGROUP_ID:8.|VARIABLE_NM:$32|OPERATOR_NM:$10|RAW_VALUE:$4000| |---|---|---|---|---|---| |AND|AND|1|AGE|=|12| |AND|AND|1|SEX|<=|'M'| |AND|OR|2|Name|NOT IN|('Jane','Alfred')| |AND|OR|2|Weight|>=|7| Rules applied: @li GROUP_LOGIC - only AND/OR @li SUBGROUP_LOGIC - only AND/OR @li SUBGROUP_ID - only integers @li VARIABLE_NM - must be in the target table @li OPERATOR_NM - only =/>/</<=/>=/BETWEEN/IN/NOT IN/NE/CONTAINS @li RAW_VALUE - no unquoted values except integers, commas and spaces.
mp_filtercheck.outputs: The &outds table containing any bad rows, plus a REASON_CD column.
mp_filtercheck.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.inds: The table to be checked, with the format above
parameters.targetds=: The target dataset against which to verify VARIABLE_NM. This must be available (ie, the library must be assigned).
parameters.abort=: (YES) If YES will call mp_abort.sas on any exceptions
parameters.outds=: The output table, which is a copy of the &inds. table plus a REASON_CD column, containing only bad records.  If bad records found, the SYSCC value will be set to 1008 (general data problem).  Downstream processes should check this table (and return code) before continuing.

mp_filtergenerate.name: mp_filtergenerate
mp_filtergenerate.description: Generates a filter clause from an input table, to a fileref
mp_filtergenerate.descriptionMore: Uses the input table to generate an output filter clause. This feature is used to create dynamic dropdowns in [Data Controller for SAS&reg]( https://datacontroller.io). The input table should be in the format below: |GROUP_LOGIC:$3|SUBGROUP_LOGIC:$3|SUBGROUP_ID:8.|VARIABLE_NM:$32|OPERATOR_NM:$10|RAW_VALUE:$4000| |---|---|---|---|---|---| |AND|AND|1|AGE|=|12| |AND|AND|1|SEX|<=|'M'| |AND|OR|2|Name|NOT IN|('Jane','Alfred')| |AND|OR|2|Weight|>=|7| Note - if the above table is received from an external client, the values should first be validated using the mp_filtercheck.sas macro to avoid risk of SQL injection. To generate the filter, run the following code: data work.filtertable; infile datalines4 dsd; input GROUP_LOGIC:$3. SUBGROUP_LOGIC:$3. SUBGROUP_ID:8. VARIABLE_NM:$32. OPERATOR_NM:$10. RAW_VALUE:$4000.; datalines4; AND,AND,1,AGE,=,12 AND,AND,1,SEX,<=,"'M'" AND,OR,2,Name,NOT IN,"('Jane','Alfred')" AND,OR,2,Weight,>=,7 ;;;; run; %mp_filtergenerate(work.filtertable,outref=myfilter) data _null_; infile myfilter; input; put _infile_; run; Will write the following query to the log: > ( >     AGE = 12 >   AND >     SEX <= 'M' > ) AND ( >     Name NOT IN ('Jane','Alfred') >   OR >     Weight >= 7 > )
mp_filtergenerate.author: Allan Bowe

parameters.inds: The input table with query values
parameters.outref=: The output fileref to contain the filter clause.  Will be created (or replaced).

mp_filterstore.name: mp_filterstore
mp_filterstore.description: Checks & Stores an input filter table and returns the Filter Key
mp_filterstore.descriptionMore: Used to generate a FILTER_RK from an input query dataset.  This process requires several permanent tables (names are configurable).  The benefit of storing query values at backend is to enable stored 'views' of filtered tables at frontend (ie, when building [SAS-Powered Apps]( https://sasapps.io)).  This macro is also used in [Data Controller for SAS]( https://datacontroller.io). A more recent feature of this macro is the ability to support filter queries on Format Catalogs.  This is achieved by adding a `-FC` suffix to the `libds` parameter - where the "ds" in this case is the catalog name.
mp_filterstore.author: Allan Bowe
